---
title: "R Episode 2 — Project Management & Reproducibility"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Create a self-contained RStudio/Positron project.
- Organize files with a consistent, reproducible layout.
- Refer to files with `here::here()` instead of `setwd()`.
- Pin package versions with `renv` so others can reproduce your environment.

**Teaching:** 30 min · **Exercises:** 20 min
:::

## Why projects?

Reproducibility starts with a **self-contained project**: one folder holding your code, data,
and outputs, with paths that work on anyone's machine. This is the difference between "it works
on my laptop" and "it works."

## Create a project

In RStudio: *File ▸ New Project ▸ New Directory ▸ New Project*. Or, reproducibly, from the
console with **usethis**:

```r
install.packages(c("usethis", "here", "renv"))
usethis::create_project("~/projects/gapminder-analysis")
```

A suggested layout:

```
gapminder-analysis/
├── gapminder-analysis.Rproj
├── data/            # raw, read-only inputs
├── R/               # functions and scripts
├── analysis/        # .qmd reports
├── output/          # generated figures/tables (git-ignored)
└── renv.lock        # pinned dependencies
```

## Stop using setwd(): use here()

Hard-coded paths like `setwd("/Users/me/...")` break on every other machine. Instead, **`here`**
builds paths relative to the project root:

```r
library(here)
here()                              # the project root
read_csv(here("data", "gapminder.csv"))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
This is the single most impactful reproducibility habit for new R users. Show how `here()`
returns the same correct path whether the script is run from the console, a Quarto render, or a
scheduled job — `setwd()` cannot.
:::

## Pin your environment with renv

`renv` records the exact package versions your project uses in a `renv.lock` file:

```r
renv::init()        # snapshot current packages into renv.lock
# ... install/upgrade packages as you work ...
renv::snapshot()    # update the lockfile
renv::restore()     # a collaborator reproduces your exact versions
```

Commit `renv.lock` to Git so anyone can recreate your environment.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge — Make it portable
Your script begins with `setwd("C:/Users/sam/gap")` and `read.csv("gap/data.csv")`. Rewrite it
to be portable across machines.

### Solution

```r
library(here)
library(readr)
read_csv(here("data", "data.csv"))   # no setwd, root-relative path
```
Combined with `renv::restore()` for packages, the project now runs anywhere.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- Work inside a self-contained project; keep raw data read-only.
- Use `here::here()` for paths; never `setwd()`.
- `renv` pins package versions — commit `renv.lock` for true reproducibility.
:::

Next: [Episode 3 — Seeking Help](r-03-seeking-help.qmd).
